title: Pressure-cooker pulled pork
subtitle: Pork shoulder under pressure for an hour, shreddable with a fork.
excerpt: Pork shoulder rubbed with paprika, cumin, and brown sugar, then pressure-cooked for an hour in cider and stock. The fork-shredding texture you get from a six-hour low oven, in a tenth of the time. Pile into buns with slaw and pickles.
sourceNotes: Pulled pork derives from American Southern barbecue, where pork shoulder is smoked over wood for 12 hours or more. The pressure-cooker adaptation skips the smoke for the convenience of an indoor weeknight version. McGee's On Food and Cooking (2004) explains why the cut works: the high collagen content in pork shoulder breaks down to gelatin at around 88°C, which the pressure cooker maintains comfortably at high pressure. The result is shreddable meat without the long oven time.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes excellently for up to three months. Shred and freeze in sauce in portion bags.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well in a 6-litre cooker. The leftovers serve a week of lunches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Refrigerate the shredded meat in its cooking liquid for up to four days; the meat stays moist this way.

content.text: Pork shoulder is the right cut. The intramuscular fat and connective tissue are what make pulled pork pull; loin or tenderloin will not work, no matter how long you cook them. Look for a piece with the fat cap on, bone-in if you can find it.

An hour at high pressure brings the shoulder to the shredding point. Twenty minutes of natural pressure release after that does the final work: the rest period inside the cooker keeps the meat juicy and finishes the collagen breakdown. Rushing the release with a quick vent gives you firm, dryer meat.
A 6-litre pressure cooker. Bone-in shoulders run larger; if your cooker is smaller, ask the butcher to cut the joint to fit.

Mix {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika, {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, {{light-brown-sugar}} of brown sugar, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, {{black-pepper}} of black pepper, and {{garlic-powder}} of garlic powder. Cut the pork into four large chunks. Rub the spice mix all over them, pressing it into the surface. Set the cooker to sauté, warm {{vegetable-oil}} of oil, and brown the pieces on all sides — about 3 minutes a side. Work in two batches if needed. Transfer to a plate.

Add the chopped onion to the cooker. Cook for 3 minutes until softened. Pour in {{cider-dry}} of dry cider and scrape the base with a wooden spoon to lift any browned bits. Add {{stock-chicken}} of hot chicken stock and {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar. Return the pork chunks and any resting juices to the pot.

Lock the lid. Cook at high pressure for 60 minutes. Let the pressure release naturally for 20 minutes; do not quick-release. The 20-minute natural release does as much for the texture as the hour under pressure. Open the lid carefully.

Lift the pork onto a board. Shred it with two forks, pulling the fibres apart in the direction of the grain. Strain the cooking liquid into a jug and skim the fat off the top. Pour about half the strained liquid back over the shredded meat and stir to moisten. Taste; add more cooking liquid if dry, more salt if needed. Serve in soft rolls with coleslaw and pickled red onions.

items.cause: Used loin instead of shoulder, or natural-released too briefly.
items.fix: Shoulder is non-negotiable. Always natural-release for at least 20 minutes; the meat reabsorbs juices during the rest.

items.symptom: Pork comes out firm and difficult to shred
items.cause: Cooking time too short, especially with bone-in pieces.
items.fix: Reseal and pressure-cook for another 20 minutes, then natural-release. Bone-in joints take longer than boneless.

items.cause: Heat too high or pan-fried too long.
items.fix: Brown over medium heat, not maximum. A dark crust is fine; a black one is bitter. Three minutes a side is enough.

items.cause: Brown sugar in the rub plus cider can over-sweeten.
items.fix: Add another tablespoon of cider vinegar to the cooking liquid before mixing it back into the shredded meat.

items.cause: Browned bits or rub stuck to the base.
items.fix: Always deglaze thoroughly with the cider and a wooden spoon before adding the rest of the liquid. The sugar in the rub catches if left.

content.text: The pork is even better the next day. Refrigerate the shredded meat in some of its cooking liquid to keep it moist. Freeze in portion-sized bags for up to three months; defrost overnight and reheat with a splash of stock or barbecue sauce.

Pulled pork crossed the Atlantic in the early 2000s, riding the rise of UK barbecue culture. The traditional version uses a smoker and 12 hours of low heat over hickory or oak. The pressure-cooker version trades the smoke for the convenience of an indoor cook on a weeknight; the texture is identical, the flavour profile sweeter and tangier rather than smoky. Pair with a vinegar-based slaw, soft rolls, and a sharp pickle to cut the richness.
